---
title: "Show the Right Numbers"
execute:
  warning: false
  message: false
---

This chapter follows [Chapter 4 of @Healy2026](https://socviz.co/04-group-facet-transform.html), translating the main plots to `plotnine_polars`.
The main ideas carry over directly: grouping tells a geom how observations belong together, faceting splits one plot into a set of comparable panels, and several geoms compute summaries before drawing anything.

```{python}
import polars as pl
from mizani.formatters import currency_format, percent_format
from plotnine.data import midwest as midwest_pd
from plotnine_polars import aes, after_stat
from socviz_pl import load_data
```

```{python}
gapminder = pl.read_csv(
    "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/jennybc/gapminder/main/inst/extdata/gapminder.tsv",
    separator="\t"
)
gss_sm = load_data("gss_sm")
midwest = pl.from_pandas(midwest_pd)
titanic = load_data("titanic")
oecd_sum = load_data("oecd_sum")
```

## Grouped Data and the Group Aesthetic

As in R, the line geom needs to know which observations should be connected.
With the fluent API, the `group` mapping goes inside `.geom_line()` because it applies to the line layer.

```{python}
#| label: fig-ch-04-gapminder-lines-wrong
#| fig-cap: "Connecting GDP per capita observations without a country grouping."
(
    gapminder
    .ggplot(aes(x="year", y="gdpPercap"))
    .geom_line()
)
```

```{python}
#| label: fig-ch-04-gapminder-lines
#| fig-cap: "GDP per capita over time, grouped by country."
(
    gapminder
    .ggplot(aes(x="year", y="gdpPercap"))
    .geom_line(aes(group="country"))
)
```

## Facet to Make Small Multiples

In R, the first argument to `facet_wrap()` is written as a one-sided formula, `~ continent`.
In `plotnine_polars`, we pass the column name as a string.

```{python}
#| label: fig-ch-04-facet-continent
#| fig-cap: "Faceting by continent."
(
    gapminder
    .ggplot(aes(x="year", y="gdpPercap"))
    .geom_line(aes(group="country"))
    .facet_wrap("continent")
)
```

The color name `gray70` used in R is not understood by matplotlib, which is underneath plotnine.
The hex color `#B3B3B3` is the equivalent shade.

```{python}
#| label: fig-ch-04-facet-continent-polished
#| fig-cap: "Faceting by continent with smoother and log-scaled GDP per capita."
(
    gapminder
    .ggplot(aes(x="year", y="gdpPercap"))
    .geom_line(aes(group="country"), color="#B3B3B3")
    .geom_smooth(size=1.1, method="loess", se=False)
    .scale_y_log10(labels=currency_format(precision=0, big_mark=","))
    .facet_wrap("continent", ncol=5)
    .labs(
        x="Year",
        y="GDP per capita",
        title="GDP per capita on Five Continents"
    )
)
```

For `facet_grid()`, the R formula `sex ~ race` becomes explicit `rows` and `cols` arguments.

```{python}
#| label: fig-ch-04-facet-grid
#| fig-cap: "Faceting on two categorical variables."
(
    gss_sm
    .ggplot(aes(x="age", y="childs"))
    .geom_point(alpha=0.2)
    .geom_smooth()
    .facet_grid(rows="sex", cols="race")
)
```

## Geoms Can Transform Data

`geom_bar()` counts observations before drawing bars.
This example is the direct translation of the R code, with `bigregion` mapped to `x`.

```{python}
#| label: fig-ch-04-bar-counts
#| fig-cap: "A bar chart of GSS respondents by census region."
(
    gss_sm
    .ggplot(aes(x="bigregion"))
    .geom_bar()
)
```

Modern plotnine uses `after_stat("prop")` where older ggplot examples often use `..prop..`.
As in the R example, `group=1` asks for proportions over the whole dataset rather than within each x value.

```{python}
#| label: fig-ch-04-bar-proportions
#| fig-cap: "A bar chart with proportions over all census regions."
(
    gss_sm
    .ggplot(aes(x="bigregion"))
    .geom_bar(aes(y=after_stat("prop"), group=1))
    .scale_y_continuous(labels=percent_format())
)
```

Mapping `fill` changes the interior of the bars.
The fluent API uses `.add_guides(fill="none")` to suppress a redundant legend.

```{python}
#| label: fig-ch-04-religion-fill
#| fig-cap: "GSS religious preference mapped to bar fill."
(
    gss_sm
    .ggplot(aes(x="religion", fill="religion"))
    .geom_bar()
    .add_guides(fill="none")
)
```

## Frequency Plots the Slightly Awkward Way

The stacked bar chart is direct.
The `position="fill"` adjustment changes the scale from counts to within-bar proportions.

```{python}
#| label: fig-ch-04-stacked-region-religion
#| fig-cap: "A stacked bar chart of religious preference by census region."
(
    gss_sm
    .ggplot(aes(x="bigregion", fill="religion"))
    .geom_bar()
)
```

```{python}
#| label: fig-ch-04-filled-region-religion
#| fig-cap: "Using the fill position adjustment to compare proportions across regions."
(
    gss_sm
    .ggplot(aes(x="bigregion", fill="religion"))
    .geom_bar(position="fill")
    .scale_y_continuous(labels=percent_format())
)
```

The next plot mirrors the book's "not quite what we wanted" example.
Grouping by `religion` makes the proportions add up across regions for each religion rather than within each region.

```{python}
#| label: fig-ch-04-dodged-proportions
#| fig-cap: "A dodged bar chart where proportions are grouped by religion."
(
    gss_sm
    .ggplot(aes(x="bigregion", fill="religion"))
    .geom_bar(
        aes(y=after_stat("prop"), group="religion"),
        position="dodge"
    )
    .scale_y_continuous(labels=percent_format())
)
```

Faceting by region lets each panel calculate the proportions within the region.

```{python}
#| label: fig-ch-04-faceted-proportions
#| fig-cap: "Faceting religious preference proportions within region."
(
    gss_sm
    .ggplot(aes(x="religion"))
    .geom_bar(
        aes(y=after_stat("prop"), group="bigregion"),
        position="dodge"
    )
    .facet_wrap("bigregion", ncol=1)
    .scale_y_continuous(labels=percent_format())
)
```

## Histograms and Density Plots

The `midwest` data is available from `plotnine.data` as a pandas data frame, so we convert it to Polars with `pl.from_pandas()`.

```{python}
#| label: fig-ch-04-hist-area-default
#| fig-cap: "A histogram of county area using plotnine's default bin choice."
(
    midwest
    .ggplot(aes(x="area"))
    .geom_histogram()
)
```

```{python}
#| label: fig-ch-04-hist-area-bins
#| fig-cap: "The same county-area histogram using ten bins."
(
    midwest
    .ggplot(aes(x="area"))
    .geom_histogram(bins=10)
)
```

In Polars, filtering to more than one value uses `.filter(pl.col("state").is_in([...]))`.

```{python}
#| label: fig-ch-04-hist-oh-wi
#| fig-cap: "Comparing percent college educated in Ohio and Wisconsin counties."
oh_wi = ["OH", "WI"]

(
    midwest
    .filter(pl.col("state").is_in(oh_wi))
    .ggplot(aes(x="percollege", fill="state"))
    .geom_histogram(alpha=0.4, bins=20)
)
```

```{python}
#| label: fig-ch-04-density-area
#| fig-cap: "Kernel density estimate of county area."
(
    midwest
    .ggplot(aes(x="area"))
    .geom_density()
)
```

```{python}
#| label: fig-ch-04-density-state
#| fig-cap: "Comparing area distributions by state."
(
    midwest
    .ggplot(aes(x="area", fill="state", color="state"))
    .geom_density(alpha=0.3)
)
```

The density statistic also exposes computed values.
Here `after_stat("scaled")` is the plotnine form of the ggplot idiom `..scaled..`.

```{python}
#| label: fig-ch-04-density-scaled
#| fig-cap: "Scaled density estimates for Ohio and Wisconsin counties."
(
    midwest
    .filter(pl.col("state").is_in(oh_wi))
    .ggplot(aes(x="area", fill="state", color="state"))
    .geom_density(aes(y=after_stat("scaled")), alpha=0.3)
)
```

## Avoid Transformations When Necessary

When the data already contains the values to draw, use `.geom_col()`.
It is the convenient form of `geom_bar(stat = "identity")`.

```{python}
#| label: fig-ch-04-titanic
#| fig-cap: "Survival on the Titanic, by sex."
(
    titanic
    .ggplot(aes(x="fate", y="percent", fill="sex"))
    .geom_col(position="dodge")
    .add_theme(legend_position="top")
)
```

```{python}
#| label: fig-ch-04-oecd-gap
#| fig-cap: "Using geom_col() to plot negative and positive values in a bar chart."
(
    oecd_sum
    .ggplot(aes(x="year", y="diff", fill="hi_lo"))
    .geom_col()
    .add_guides(fill="none")
    .labs(
        x=None,
        y="Difference in Years",
        title="The US Life Expectancy Gap",
        subtitle="Difference between US and OECD average life expectancies",
        caption="Data: OECD. After a chart by Christopher Ingraham."
    )
)
```

## Where to Go Next

The exercises in @Healy2026 mostly carry over.
The main translation points are to use strings instead of formulas for facets, `after_stat()` for computed statistics, and Polars expressions such as `.filter(pl.col("state").is_in(oh_wi))` for data preparation.
